title: Dog Open Sore on Paw Not Healing: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's paw sore isn't healing, the urgency of veterinary care, and how clear photos help our triage team assess the wound.
content_html:
TL;DR: A non-healing paw sore requires veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours to prevent deep-tissue infection or identify potential tumors. Seek immediate care if your dog is limping or the paw is hot and swollen.

What is a non-healing paw sore on my dog?

  • A non-healing paw sore is an open wound that fails to close or improve over several days, often caused by lick granulomas, cysts, or infections.
  • Foreign bodies, such as grass seeds lodged in the skin, or certain types of skin tumors can also prevent proper healing.
  • Dogs frequently lick these areas for comfort, which introduces more bacteria and prevents the tissue from knitting back together.

Is a non-healing paw sore a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium, requiring veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Delaying care can lead to serious complications like deep-tissue infections, cellulitis, or the spread of malignant growths.
  • You should seek care sooner if your dog is limping, lethargic, or the paw feels significantly swollen and hot to the touch.

How do photos of the lesion help the triage team?

  • Providing a high-quality, well-lit photograph allows veterinary professionals to assess the color, discharge, and borders of the sore.
  • Visual details help the team differentiate between a simple abrasion and a more complex growth.
  • Clear images ensure your pet is prioritized correctly and that you receive the most accurate advice before arriving at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Delayed wound healing in dogs with open sores on the paw can be attributed to several factors. Low tissue oxygen levels, potentially due to hypovolemia, devitalized tissue, hematomas, or excessively tight bandages, can interfere with protein synthesis and fibroblast activity. Endogenous factors such as anemia and hypoproteinemia (serum protein <2 g/dL) also impede healing. Malnutrition can alter the healing process, while supplementation with DL-methionine or cysteine may help reverse the delay. Uremia can also interfere with wound healing. Open wound management may be appropriate when primary closure is not suitable due to gross contamination or infection. In cases of interdigital palmar and plantar comedones and follicular cysts, laser therapy may be considered. For chronic lesions where the inciting cause is idiopathic, or cannot be treated or resolved, pulse or chronic low-dose antibiotic therapy may provide clinical remission and pain relief.

Chapter: General Principles, Wound Management

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1707)

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ointments on my dog's paw?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human ointments like Neosporin unless directed by a vet, as many contain ingredients that can be toxic if your dog licks them off.

faqs.question: Why does my dog keep licking the sore?
faqs.answer: Dogs lick wounds due to instinct, but it often causes 'lick granulomas,' where the repetitive friction prevents healing and causes more inflammation.

faqs.question: Should I bandage the paw at home?
faqs.answer: Generally, it is safer to use an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone') to stop licking rather than bandaging, as improper bandaging can cut off circulation or trap moisture.
